narrative:
We remain under the influence of high pressure centered in the western Atlantic. Friday is warm and pleasant, though a renegade afternoon shower is possible. A front moves through from the west Saturday afternoon with showers (thunder possible), and a shower or two may linger into Saturday night. Temperatures are less warm early next week. A stray shower cannot be ruled out Sunday morning, but Monday is dry. We transition out of this warm pattern next week as more active weather develops.

2027 Blue Ridge Calendar Photo Contest

Voting is open for the 2027 photo contest.

We received 854 photos from 211 photographers in the 2027 Blue Ridge Calendar Photo Contest. From those, our five judges chose 20 finalists from each season from 53 photographers. You can vote every day. The voting period closes May 3.

Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android

Subscribe Forecast Discussion High pressure in the western Atlantic controls our weather through Friday, but a front moves through Saturday afternoon, nudging it eastward. We have a warm, pleasant Friday with a “barely worth mentioning” potential for an afternoon shower. Clouds increase Saturday, with showers in the afternoon and possibly lingering into the evening. Morning sun still gives us a seasonably warm Saturday before the showers arrive. The front is slow to exit the Carolinas Sunday, so a stray shower remains possible into Sunday morning. As the front edges southward, Monday is dry. Cooler (more seasonal) air works into our region briefly early next week. Late Monday night, the front returns north as a warm front, with showers again possible Tuesday morning. A more potent front arrives Wednesday. Expect scattered showers and thundershowers, mostly in the afternoon. This is our last warm day for a while. Cooler air arrives Thursday with decreasing clouds. We’ll have another opportunity for rain later next week. Detailed forecast discussion and analysis is only available to RaysWeather supporters. All Photos Submit Photo Latest Insights Monday Message for April 20, 2026 This week's roller-coaster temperature ride continues with more dry conditions perpetuating the drought. As we look further ahead, there may be better news on sending western moisture our way for week two. Apr 20, 2026 February 2026 Recap Monthly Recap Not-So-Frigid February Unlike January, which followed a more “trackable” pattern of starting mild and ending cold, February was a little more helter-skelter, with a cold and snowy start, followed by an interrupted, generally mild pattern over weeks two through four. Not to say that February was a mild month, that would be a mischaracterization. Most of the final numbers ended up near to slightly above (think 2 or 3 degrees) average. The statewide snowstorm that wrapped up January bled into the 1st of February for those in the mountains, and a largely chilly first 10 days of the month delivered two more snow events, with a light event in the Foothills on the 5th and a light/moderate northwest flow event on the 6th & 7th. The first 10 days of the month all had below freezing lows in Hickory, which totaled 18 consecutive freezes when going back into late January. Nothing out of the ordinary, but a good sign of long-lasting cold. On the 10th itself, a quietly remarkable occurrence unfolded in the Foothills, as morning lows in the mid to upper 20s preceded afternoon highs in the low 70s. Temperature swings of above 40 degrees are a rarity around here, and Edgemont’s 46 degree swing from morning to afternoon is not something you see often at all! A fairly benign period followed through the 20th, with the only notable occurrence being a widespread rain event on Sunday the 15th, when several stations recorded an inch or more of rain as a moist low pressure system cut northeastward through the region. This was the heaviest rain of the month at all stations, with a lighter event on the 26th delivering generally a half inch or less to most. Before that event, we had a fairly heavy northwest flow snow from the 22nd to the 24th that produced multiple reports of 9” or greater at high elevations, with double digit totals at the ski resorts, Roan Mountain, and Wolf Laurel. High temperatures in the Foothills failed to escape the 30s on the 23rd, and flurries flew down to the South Carolina state line for much of the day. After that, the month went out quietly, with mildly above normal temperatures. Local Details Slightly Above Normal Temperatures

Precipitation was mostly below normal once again, marking the fourth straight month that much of the region experienced drier than average conditions. The Western Escarpment had better luck with a few northwest flow snow events, while the Foothills and SWNC stayed drier, as you can see from the map below. This all plays into the drought concerns as spring wears on. Apr 24, 2026 January 2026 Recap Monthly Recap Snowy and Cold (Mostly) Remember when January started out so mild? From the 2nd to the 10th, Beech Mountain had 9 consecutive high temperatures above 40 degrees and 7 low temperatures that exceeded the average high temperature for those dates! But such a warm start is easily forgotten when followed quickly by the cold, ice, wind, and snow that dominated the second half of January 2026. A cold front on the 11th kicked off a string of 6 different snow events in the final 20 days of the month, of varying degrees of intensity and coverage. Everyone in the Ray’s Weather coverage region will recall the back-to-back weekend winter storms, with the icy mess around the 24th, and the widespread “Murphy to Manteo” snowstorm that delivered 1” or more to every square mile of North Carolina on the 31st. Thanks to the North Carolina Climate Office and National Weather Service for the statewide map below. With a layer of icy mess underneath this widespread snowfall, the staying power of the snowcover was incredible, lasting well into February. Several days of school cancelled were across the region throughout that period. The second-half winter rally erased the above-normal start, and then some. While the overall departure for the entire month was not impressively below normal, that smoothed-out number does not tell the story like y’all remember it, which I presume is "cold and snowy." Sugar Mountain dropped below zero three separate times, hitting -5 degrees on the 24th, 26th, and 31st. Local Details January was a fairly dry month, with three notable, widespread precipitation events. The two winter storms that we already mentioned, and a rather moist cold front that kicked off the wintry stretch around the 10th & 11th. Several stations recorded over an inch of rain, with 1.52” at North Wilkesboro, 1.02” at East Boone, and over 2” at Waynesville. A slew of northwest flow snow events would precede the ice storm later in the month. One following the rainy cold front on the 11th (link), a wind-blown event on the 14th (link), and a light event on the 17th (link). Despite these events, the Western Escarpment region found itself experiencing a more below-normal month of precipitation than the eastern mountain and western Foothill regions. The tables below give you all the details you need for January around here:
